coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode nanosensor for sensitive detection of methotrexate based on fluorescence technology and deep learning algorithms

coredata.dc:description:
Background Methotrexate (MTX) in the body can result in severe, potentially life-threatening side effects. As a result, it is imperative to establish a dependable, precise, and specific method for detecting MTX. However, conventional sensors often suffer from limitations such as low sensitivity, prolonged response times, and insufficient accuracy.
Result To solve this problem, we propose a novel nanoscale double-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor (N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs) that leverages the unique properties of nanomaterials for enhanced detection performance. This sensor integrates sulfhydryl-modified mesoporous silica-encapsulated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs@mSiO2-SH) with bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters (GSH@AuAg NCs), creating a nanoscale platform with dual emission peaks at 460 nm and 600 nm under 370 nm excitation. The introduction of MTX triggers a distinct fluorescence response: the intensity increases at 460 nm while decreases at 600 nm, accompanied by a visible color change from orange-red to blue. This phenomenon is attributed to the interaction between the carboxyl groups in MTX and the GSH@AuAg NCs, which disrupts the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between the N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and GSH@AuAg NCs.
Significance The sensor has an impressive wide linear range of 5.00–500.00 μM and a low detection limit of 1.75 μM. In addition, a deep learning algorithm is utilized to predict concentration levels in real-time images, enabling rapid and accurate monitoring of MTX levels in complex detection scenarios.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode nanosensor for sensitive detection of methotrexate based on fluorescence technology and deep learning algorithms

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methotrexate (MTX) in the body can result in severe, potentially life-threatening side effects. As a result, it is imperative to establish a dependable, precise, and specific method for detecting MTX. However, conventional sensors often suffer from limitations such as low sensitivity, prolonged response times, and insufficient accuracy.

To solve this problem, we propose a novel nanoscale double-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor (N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs) that leverages the unique properties of nanomaterials for enhanced detection performance. This sensor integrates sulfhydryl-modified mesoporous silica-encapsulated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs@mSiO2-SH) with bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters (GSH@AuAg NCs), creating a nanoscale platform with dual emission peaks at 460 nm and 600 nm under 370 nm excitation. The introduction of MTX triggers a distinct fluorescence response: the intensity increases at 460 nm while decreases at 600 nm, accompanied by a visible color change from orange-red to blue. This phenomenon is attributed to the interaction between the carboxyl groups in MTX and the GSH@AuAg NCs, which disrupts the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between the N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and GSH@AuAg NCs.

The sensor has an impressive wide linear range of 5.00–500.00 μM and a low detection limit of 1.75 μM. In addition, a deep learning algorithm is utilized to predict concentration levels in real-time images, enabling rapid and accurate monitoring of MTX levels in complex detection scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methotrexate (MTX), a folate analogue and antagonist, is extensively used in the therapy of various conditions, including cancers like acute leukemia, neck cancer, and osteosarcoma [1–3]. When administered in excessive amounts, MTX is excreted into the environment via urine and feces [4,5]. Due to its chemical stability, MTX is resistant to degradation, leading to its prolonged presence in environmental media such as water and soil [6]. This persistence can have deleterious effects on aquatic organisms and soil microorganisms, disrupting ecosystem functions [7]. Moreover, the environmental stability of MTX facilitates its uptake by plants, particularly in contaminated soil or through irrigation with polluted water. As a result, MTX can enter the food chain, posing a potential risk of human exposure through the consumption of contaminated vegetables. This underscores the critical need for robust monitoring of MTX levels in agricultural environments to mitigate potential public health risks. Various methods have been developed for MTX detection in recent years [8], including electrochemical (EC) sensors, molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) sensors, chromatographic techniques, immunosensors, and colorimetric methods. However, each method has its limitations. For instance, EC sensors require enhancements in specificity and immunity to interference from complex sample matrices [9,10]. MIP sensors are intricate to prepare and may face challenges in practical applications due to template regeneration and selective inactivation. Chromatographic techniques provide high resolution and accuracy in MTX detection but demand complex operations, expensive equipment, and lengthy analysis cycles, rendering them unsuitable for swift field detection [11]. Immunosensors are constrained by antibody stability and specificity, and are easily influenced by sample matrices, potentially leading to biased outcomes [12]. Colorimetric method may not be ideal for highly sensitive MTX detection in complex sample matrices [13]. It is increasingly important to develop a method with high sensitivity, excellent specificity, and strong anti-interference ability to effectively detect MTX in complex environments.

Currently, fluorescent nanosensors are attracting increasing attention from researchers due to their optical stability, low cost, and high detection accuracy. Carbon dots (CDs) are particularly suitable for use as biomarkers and fluorescent sensors due to their nanoscale dimensions, high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) [14] and excellent photostability [15]. Additionally, CDs have a very functional surface, which makes them very amenable to chemical modification, allowing precise control of the fluorescence properties and enhancing biocompatibility and specific recognition capabilities [16]. To enhance the immunity and selectivity of the sensor, CDs are embedded in mesoporous silica (mSiO2) to protect them from the external environment, ensuring efficient performance in complex biological or environmental samples [17]. Using silicon as a carrier for CDs not only minimally impacts fluorescence but also enhances the efficiency and stability of carbon dots, leading to a significant extension of their service life and stability [18]. Bimetallic nanoclusters, as an emerging nanomaterial, combine the excellent properties of two different metas [19]. Compared to metal nanoclusters (MNCs), bimetallic nanoclusters not only exhibit an excellent surface-enhanced Raman scattering effect but also a wider spectral response range, further enhancing the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensors [20]. At present, researchers have developed a dual-emission ratio fluorescence nanosensor by combining CDs and MNCs [21,22]. This sensor offers improved sensitivity and selectivity compared to single-emission fluorescence sensors by leveraging the strong fluorescence properties of CDs and the specific recognition abilities of MNCs [23,24]. This nanosensor measures the ratio of fluorescence signals at two different wavelengths, making it ideal for precise monitoring of target molecules in complex samples. More importantly, dual-emission fluorescence sensors offer superior self-correction and a wide range of colour options, therefore become a more suitable choice for visual inspection than single-emission fluorescence sensors. Besides, many researchers employ visual observation and red-green-blue (RGB) strategies to improve the performance of fluorescent sensors for applications [25]. By employing the visual observation and RGB strategy, researchers successfully obtained multi-color fluorescence responses in the sensor. But both visual observation and the RGB strategy have limitations. Visual observation could not easily detect subtle color changes, especially with large sample size data. The RGB strategy can only learn linear relationships in data, and could not adequately mine data features under large sample sizes [26]. Therefore, a more efficient method is required for analyzing large sample data in order to achieve effective detection in complex environments.

To tackle this challenge, this paper proposes a new prediction algorithm by combining deep learning model with fluorescence nanosensors, this algorithm can achieve end-to-end prediction from experimental images to concentrations. The algorithm contains two stages. In the feature extraction stage, we build a network based on the AlexNet structure. AlexNet was first proposed by Hinton and Alex Krizhevsky in 2012, which added advanced training techniques like nonlinear rectified linear unit (ReLU) activation function, dual graphic processing unit (GPU) training and local response normalisation (LRN) into traditional convolutional network for the first time [27]. AlexNet has become an excellent image feature extraction and classification model in computer vision field since it was first proposed [28]. In the feature mapping stage, we build a four-layer fully-connected network to bring the feature space into the concentration space, the concentrations of fluorescent substances can be predicted by features which extracted previously. Compared with the traditional linear regression prediction model, the deep learning algorithm proposed by this paper can achieve end-to-end learning, with short running time and excellent non-linear relationships fitting performance, thus improves the accuracy and stability of concentration prediction.

In this work, gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters (GSH@AgAu NCs) were assembled with sulfhydryl-modified mesoporous silicon-encapsulated carbon dots (N-CDs@mSiO2) to create nanoscale fluorescent nanosensors. The nanostructured mSiO2 encapsulated the N-CDs to act as an inner reference signal for fluorescence, while the external signal was provided by nanoscale GSH@AgAu NCs. When excited at 370 nm, the nanosensor emitted light at two different wavelengths. After interaction with MTX, the nanosensor emission strength at 600 nm declined while the emission strength at 460 nm was elevated. This change was attributed to the formation of a nanoscale complex between Au in the AgAu NCs and the carboxyl group in MTX, which interfered with the FRET process between N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and GSH@AuAg NCs, leading to fluorescence recovery at 460 nm. The synthesis process of nanoscale N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Furthermore, the deep learning algorithm was utilized to predict concentration levels under real time images, allowing for rapid and precise monitoring of MTX levels in complex detection scenarios.

The mSiO2 was synthesized based on a previously reported method with slight modifications [29]. First, 6.5 g of CTAC and 0.3 mL of TEA were dissolved in 60 mL of water and stirred at 65 °C for 1 h in a 100 mL round-bottom flask. Next, 4.5 mL of TEOS dissolved in 20 mL of cyclohexane was slowly introduced into the solution, and the mixture was allowed to react overnight at 65 °C in an oil bath with magnetic stirring at 180 rpm. After the reaction, the product was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with ethanol to eliminate any unreacted substances. To ensure complete removal of the template, the final product was calcined at 500 °C for 6 h in air.

CA (1.0 g) and EDA (5.0 mL) were combined with 25.0 mL water and sonicated for 30 min to a uniform solution. The mSiO2 (1.0 g) was then added to this solution and sonicated for 30 min. To form N-CDs@mSiO2, the mixture was poured into a 50 mL teflon vessel and heated at 180 °C for 1 h. Finally, the resulting product was purified for 24 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3000 Da and then lyophilized to a solid. A quantity of 0.5 g of the product was added to 30.0 mL of EtOH, and the solution was subjected to sonication in order to ensure uniform dispersion. 200 μL MPTMS was added and stirred, then refluxed at 70 °C for 6 h in N2 atmosphere. Finally, the reaction mixture was fully washed with EtOH and dried to obtain N-CDs@mSiO2-SH. The quantum yields (QYs) of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH were determined using 0.1 M quinine sulphate. The QYs were calculated using the following equation:

The GSH@AuAg NCs nanocomposites were prepared according to a previously reported method [30]. Initially, solutions of HAuCl4·3H2O (20 mM, 9 mL), AgNO3 (20 mM, 1 mL), and GSH (20 mM, 7.5 mL) were prepared and then mixed with 37.5 mL of water. The mixture was gently stirred at room temperature for 1 h, followed by heating at 85 °C for 3 h, resulting in a color change to yellow then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 200 μL of GSH@AuAg NCs (15 mg/mL) nanocomposites were stirred at 30 °C. Subsequently, 500 μL of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH (5 mg/mL) solution was added and the mixture was stirred at 30 °C for 3 h. The resulting N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs product was collected by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min and washed thrice with ultrapure water. The final product was dried in a vacuum drying oven to obtain N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs.

To verify the successful assembly of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs, a combination of centrifugation and fluorescence spectral analysis was used. Specifically, 0.2 g of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs were dispersed in 20 mL of ultrapure water and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was then collected for analysis by fluorescence spectral analysis. The results showed almost no detectable fluorescence signal in the supernatant, indicating that N-CDs were effectively encapsulated within the mSiO2 and the AuAg NCs were successfully anchored on the surface of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH. These results confirm the successful assembly of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT and imaging experiments of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa cells were initially seeded into 96-well culture plates, followed by aspiration of the old culture medium and four subsequent washes. Samples at varying concentrations were then introduced into the medium and incubated for approximately 24 h. Next, 15 μL of the 0.3 mg/mL reagent was incorporated for a 5-h incubation period. After aspirating the supernatant, 100 μL of DMSO solution was added, and the absorbance was measured using an enzyme marker after 10 min. HeLa cells were initially seeded at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells per 35 mm culture plate and allowed to incubate overnight. Subsequently, 100 μL of an aqueous solution containing N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs (6.0 mg/mL) was combined with 2.0 mL of the culture medium to attain a concentration of 0.3 mg/mL for N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs. Following a 0.5-h incubation period, the cells were washed. A laser confocal microscope was utilized to conduct optical bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of MTX, selectivity of the developed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
A 1.0 mL solution of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs (5.0 mg/mL) was combined with 4.0 mL of 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.0. Different concentrations of MTX (5.0 μL) were then added to this mixture. The fluorescence intensity ratio of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs was measured and recorded at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm (F
600
/F
460 ). To determine the MTX levels in hospital wastewater, samples were collected from Shanxi Provincial Tumour Hospital. These samples were centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min. The supernatant was then tested using the same procedure described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deep learning algorithm for MTX concentration prediction
ce:section.ce:para: This section proposes a novel predictive model for MTX concentration using a deep learning algorithm. The experimental phase included the acquisition of 250 images with their corresponding concentration values. To improve the model's robustness and avoid overfitting, image augmentation technique was applied during data preprocessing. The augmented images were then used as inputs to the model. In the feature extraction stage, a network based on the AlexNet structure was employed, including five convolutional layers to extract image features from low-level to high-level abstractions. In the feature mapping stage, a four-layer fully-connected network was constructed to predict concentrations based on the image feature vectors.

In Fig. 2 a and b both N-CDs and GSH@AuAg NCs exhibit a spherical morphology and are uniformly distributed in water. Fig. 2a & b insets are analysed using a Gaussian distribution, which shows that the grain size distributions of N-CDs and GSH@AuAg NCs are between 0.75 to 3.25 nm and 0.50–5.50 nm, respectively, with mean particle sizes of 2.50 nm for N-CDs and 1.75 nm for GSH@AuAg NCs. Furthermore, the HRTEM image illustrates that the N-CDs and GSH@AuAg NCs exhibit lattice spacings of 0.153 nm and 0.128 nm, respectively. The crystalline properties of CDs are primarily attributed to nanodomains of sp2-hybridised carbon, which exhibit varying lattice spacings [31]. The in-plane lattice fringe spacing of CDs ranges from 0.18 to 0.25 nm, consistent with the (100) lattice plane spacing of graphite (0.21 nm) [32]. However, nitrogen (N) doping can induce lattice contraction, leading to a reduction in the size of the luminescent cores and a reduction in the lattice fringe spacing to approximately 0.153 nm [33]. The lattice spacing of 0.128 nm in GSH@AuAg NCs corresponds to the (220) crystal plane of the Au–Ag bimetallic alloy, reflecting its. unique alloy structure and crystallographic properties [34,35]. Fig. 2c & d illustrate the SEM images of mSiO2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs nanocomposites, respectively. The mSiO2 shows highly uniform highly mesoporous nanospheres with an average particle size of 160 nm. However, the average particle size of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs nanocomposites becomes 170 nm due to the attachment of nanoclusters on the surface of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs. In Fig. 2e & f, TEM images and HETEM images show that mSiO2 is filled with a large number of N-CDs, while GSH@AuAg NCs are bound to the upper surface of the N-CDs@mSiO2. The nanocomposites N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs illustrated in Fig. 2e demonstrate a well-defined mesoporous structure with a central-radial configuration and show remarkable dispersion in water. Additionally, TEM elemental mapping images (Fig. 2g) demonstrate a homogeneous and random distribution of silicon (Si), gold (Au), oxygen (O), silver (Ag), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and total elements within the nanocomposites. The EDX-mapping indicates that these nanocomposites primarily consist of six elements: Si (Fig. 2h), O (Fig. 2i), N (Fig. 2j), S (Fig. 2k), Au (Fig. 2l), Ag (Fig. 2m) and total elements (Fig. 2n). TEOS and MPTMS are the main sources of Si and O respectively. The N is mainly derived from the N-CDs, while the S is derived from both the MPTMS and the GSH@AuAg NCs. The metallic elements Au and Ag are contributed exclusively by the GSH@AuAg NCs nanoparticles.

In Fig. 3 a, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs verifies the presence of elements including carbon, silicon, oxygen, gold, nitrogen, sulfur, and silver. The O1s spectrum in Fig. 3b demonstrates two distinct peaks at 532.30 eV (O–NO/O–Si/O–H) and 531.80 eV (OC/O–SO). Similarly, the Si2p spectrum in Fig. 3c identifies peaks at 103.70 eV, 103.00 eV, and 102.40 eV, corresponding to SiO2, Si–N, and Si–O/Si–C bonds. The S2p spectrum in Fig. 3d reveals peaks at 164.20 eV (S 2p1/2), 163.60 eV (S 2p3/2), and 160.00 eV (S–Ag/S–Au). Furthermore, Fig. 3e presents the XPS Au spectrum with peaks at 87.55 and 83.90 eV, attributed to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2, confirming the presence of Au (I) and Au (0). The XPS Ag spectrum in Fig. 3f shows peaks at 373.85 and 367.90 eV, corresponding to Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2. The bands between 976 and 1440 cm−1 indicate the polymerization of Si–OH groups, resulting in the formation of Si–O–Si bonds within cyclic structures. Additionally, a weak band at 822 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of Si–O bonds in mesoporous silica. The broad absorption band between 521 and 766 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the Si–O bonds, while the band at 450 cm−1 is assigned to the Si–O bending vibration. The N-CDs@mSiO2-SH in Fig. S2 exhibit stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H groups, which are indicated by a new broad absorption band in the range of 2870–3070 cm−1. The presence of carbonyl functional groups in N-CDs@mSiO2-SH is inferred from the sharp absorption peaks at 1480 and 1590 cm−1. Additionally, the formation of C–N/C–O bonds is supported by absorption peaks at 1300 cm−1. Furthermore, a broad absorption band in the range of 2050–2380 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the –SH groups. The N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs shown in Fig. 4 a exhibit nearly all of the mentioned peaks. However, the –SH stretching vibration peaks at 2050-2380 cm−1 of the N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs, preparing subsequent to the introduction of AuAg NCs, and show a notable decrease in intensity compared to those of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH. The incorporation of AuAg NCs suggests the formation of an Au–S coordination bond with the sulfhydryl group (-SH) of MPTMS, resulting in a decrease in the absorption intensity of the –SH stretching vibration peaks [36].

The results of the dynamic light scattering (DLS) investigation in Fig. S3 demonstrates that the mSiO2 nanospheres display highly uniform hydrodynamic size distributions in aqueous solution with a low polydispersity index (PDI = 0.05). The average hydrodynamic size of the nanospheres is approximately 150 nm. After assembling N-CDs and GSH@AuAg NCs, the average hydrodynamic diameter of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs is 163 nm as shown in Fig. 4b, indicating a particle size increase of over 10 nm. Fig. 4c illustrates the Zeta potentials of different nanoparticles. The mSiO2 nanoparticles have a negative charge of −18.33 mV, while the N-CDs@mSiO2 show a partially positive surface with a zeta potential of 3.20 mV. The Zeta potential of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH is −13.63 mV, resulting from the interaction between positively charged N-CDs@mSiO2 and negatively charged sulfhydryl groups. GSH@AuAg NCs have a Zeta potential of 3.60 mV, whereas N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs exhibit a Zeta potential of −8.64 mV, indicating the aggregation of GSH@AuAg NCs and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH to form nanocomposites via electrostatic assembly. The study delves into the optical properties of nanocomposites, specifically examining distinct spectra of fluorescence emission of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and GSH@AuAg NCs when exposed to different excitations from 300 to 390 nm. In Fig. S4 the emission peak for N-CDs@mSiO2-SH is observed at 460 nm, while GSH@AuAg NCs in Fig. S5 exhibited a peak at 600 nm. Interestingly, these peaks are found to be independent of the excitation wavelength. The maximum excitation/emission values for N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and GSH@AuAg NCs are recorded at 370/460 nm and 370/600 nm, respectively. Notably, when excited at 370 nm, the N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs display dual emission peaks at 460 and 600 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 4d. Furthermore, the inset of Fig. 4d illustrates the visual characteristics of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs when exposed to sunlight and UV lamp conditions. The nanocomposites appear light brown in sunlight but show an orange-red color when subjected to UV light. To improve the performance of the fluorescence sensor developed, the effect of pH on N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and GSH@AuAg NCs is examined separately, as shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7. It is observed that the fluorescence maximum is at pH 7. To further investigate the stability of GSH@AgAu NCs and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs, we evaluated their fluorescence intensity under continuous illumination and prolonged storage. Specifically, both nanomaterials were subjected to sustained light irradiation for a duration of 48 h, during which their fluorescence intensities were monitored over time. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of GSH@AgAu NCs at 600 nm remained nearly unchanged, whereas N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs exhibited a stable fluorescence intensity ratio at 460 nm and 600 nm, as illustrated in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9. Additionally, the long-term stability of both nanomaterials was assessed over a period of 30 days by tracking their fluorescence intensities. The findings demonstrate that both GSH@AgAu NCs and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs maintained excellent fluorescence stability throughout the study period, as depicted in Fig. S10 and Fig. S11.

Fig. 3e–g illustrate the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms, with the specific surface area determined using the multi-point brunauer-emmett-teller (BET) method. The variation in pore size distribution was analysed through barrett-joyner-halenda (BJH) analysis. An inflection point around 0.9 P/P0 indicates a type II adsorption-desorption curve for these particles. The surface area as measured by BET and the pore volume of mSiO2 are approximately 651.87 m2/g and 2.05 cm3/g, respectively. In the case of N-CDs@mSiO2, the BET surface area and pore volume measure 393.90 m2/g and 1.23 cm3/g, respectively. When examining N-CDs@mSiO2-NH2@AuAg NCs, the reported values for the BET surface area and pore volume are 198.08 m2/g and 0.96 cm3/g, respectively. Both materials, whether pre-functionalized or post-functionalized, display characteristics typical of mesoporous materials. In Fig. 3h the loading capacity of N-CDs and GSH@AuAg NCs in mSiO2 was investigated using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), revealing mass loss from room temperature to 800 °C. The initial weight loss in mSiO2 nanospheres is attributed to evaporated water below 100 °C, while mass loss above 200 °C is typically due to thermal decomposition or combustion of organic material. The total mass loss of SiO2 is 5.6 %. In the case of N-CDs@mSiO2 and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs nanocomposites, weight loss mainly stems from the decomposition of N-CDs and AuAg NCs, resulting in total weight losses of 16.4 % and 48.3 %, respectively.

The emission spectra of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH, GSH@AuAg NCs and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs are analysed using an illumination wavelength of 370 nm as shown in Fig. S12. N-CDs@mSiO2-SH shows a weaker emission band at 460 nm compared to N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs. When compared to GSH@AuAg NCs, N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs display similar emission band positions at 600 nm with increased intensity. This effect is likely due to FRET or IFE (inner filter effect). Subsequently, in Fig. S13, the time-resolved spectra of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs are further examined. The average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs at Ex 370/Em 460 nm are found to be 10.19 ns (τ1 = 3.25 ns, 20.36 %; τ2 = 11.69 ns, 79.64 %) and 5.22 ns (τ1 = 2.33 ns, 27.36 %; τ2 = 6.32 ns, 72.37 %), respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH notably decreased after interaction with GSH@AuAg NCs. Furthermore, Fig. S14 illustrates a significant overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and the UV absorption spectra of GSH@AuAg NCs, indicating a high likelihood of energy transfer and an efficient FRET process between the two entities. To support this hypothesis, the distance between the acceptor and the donor was calculated according to the equation:

measured as 2.13 × 104 M−1 cm−1 nm4. By calculation, the distance is determined to be 13.53 Å, falling within the range of 10–100 Å, confirming the sensing mechanism as a FRET process. The impact of MTX on the emission spectrum of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs fluorescent nanosensors is depicted in Fig. 5 a. With increasing MTX concentration, there is a gradual increase in emission at 460 nm and a decrease at 600 nm. Additionally, Fig. S15 shows the time-resolved spectra of GSH@AuAg NCs both with and without MTX. The fluorescence lifetime of GSH@AuAg NCs remains almost constant at 8.02 ns (τ = 8.02 ns, 100 %) upon reaction with MTX, indicating a static quenching event between GSH@AuAg NCs and MTX [38]. In aqueous solution, the carboxyl (-COOH) groups of MTX undergo deprotonation to form carboxylate (-COO-) ions. The α-COOH (pKa≈3.5) and γ-COOH (pKa≈4.8) groups are more susceptible to deprotonation under mildly acidic or neutral conditions, thereby enhancing their coordination ability with metal ions [39]. The -COO- coordinate with Ag+ on the surface of AuAg NCs through the lone-pair electrons of oxygen, forming stable σ-coordination bonds [40,41]. Additionally, the amino (-NH2) groups in MTX, which possess lone-pair electrons, can interact with Au (0) on the surface of AuAg NCs via σ-coordination, where the vacant d orbitals of Au (0) accept electron density from nitrogen, thereby strengthening the interaction [42]. The pteridine ring in MTX can further interact with Au (0) through a d→π back-donation mechanism, where in the filled d orbitals of Au (0) donate electron density to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the pteridine ring, leading to d→π interactions [43]. Concurrently, the aromatic π-system of MTX interacts with the delocalized surface electrons of AuAg NCs via London dispersion forces [44]. Upon coordination of MTX carboxylate groups with Ag+, the distance between Au (0) and the pteridine ring decreases, optimizing π-overlap and further stabilizing the coordination structure [45]. Furthermore, fluorescence lifetime measurements of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs after interaction with MTX, as shown in Fig. S13, revealed a recovery of the average fluorescence lifetime to 9.55 ns (τ1 = 4.22 ns, 32.36 %; τ2 = 12.10 ns, 67.64 %). The energy transfer efficiency (E) was calculated using the equation:
E =
(
1 −
T
T 0
)
× 100 %
[46], where
T
represents the donor fluorescence lifetime in the presence of FRET, and
T 0
is the intrinsic fluorescence lifetime of the donor. The experimental results demonstrated that, when FRET occurred, the energy transfer efficiency was 48.78 %. However, upon binding with MTX, FRET was disrupted, resulting in a decrease in energy transfer efficiency to 6.28 %. These findings provide direct evidence that the coordination interaction between MTX and AuAg NCs effectively disrupts the FRET process between N-CDs@mSiO2-SH and AuAg NCs.

MTT experiments have been performed to evaluate the cell toxicity of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs fluorescent nanosensors. Cell viability is assessed using the equation: cell viability (%) = (ODTreated/ODControl) × 100 %, where ODTreated and ODControl represent optical density readings in the presence and absence of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs, respectively. In Fig. S16, it is observed that the viability of HeLa cells decreases as the concentration of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs increases. Notably, at a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL, the cell viability rate exceeded 85 %, indicating low toxicity of the nanosensors.

Cellular imaging was conducted using LS-800 to capture Fig. 5, which displays the colocalization image of Hela cells treated with 0.3 mg/mL N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs and N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs along with 500 μM of MTX. After the entry of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs into the cells, the cell morphology appears well defined and shuttle-like. The images in bright field (Fig. 5a), orange-red channel (Fig. 5b), blue channel (Fig. 5c), and merge channel (Fig. 5d) exhibit excellent staining ability, confirming the biocompatibility of the fluorescent nanosensor. Upon the addition of 500 μM MTX, all HeLa cells undergo apoptosis as observed in the bright field (Fig. 5e). The orange-red fluorescence bursts in Fig. 5f, while the blue fluorescence increases in Fig. 5g, with Fig. 5h representing the merge channel. Consequently, the orange-red fluorescence in the cells progressively bursts and the blue fluorescence intensifies with 500 μM MTX, leading to the gradual elimination of cancer cells.

Fluorescence changes of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs induced by MTX are illustrated in Fig. 6 a, showing a fluorescence boost at 460 nm (F
460 ) and quenching at 600 nm (F
600 ). The relationship between F
600
/F
460 and MTX concentrations ranging from 5.0 to 500.0 μM is depicted in Fig. 6b, demonstrating a highly linear correlation with an r-square (R2) of 0.9979. The limit of detection (LOD) is determined to be 1.75 μM, indicating the effectiveness of the nanosensor for MTX analysis. The inset image in Fig. 6a exhibits the color change under UV light with increasing MTX concentrations. Fig. 6c and d presents the ratio [(F
600
/F
460
)/(F
600
/F
460
)
0 ] of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs in the presence of potential interferents (DA, AA, VB1, SM, U, QR, MF, CAT, Cl−, F−, SCN−, K+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cys, Hcy, Gly) and MTX. The inset image in Fig. 6c demonstrates a color change from orange-red to blue under UV light only in the presence of MTX, highlighting the selectivity of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs towards MTX. Overall, the study confirms the specificity and immunity of N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs to MTX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTX concentration prediction with deep learning algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, a deep learning algorithm is developed to predict the concentration of MTX. The original experiment involves recording of 250 experimental image data. These images were captured after carefully transferring 250 solutions with varying MTX concentrations into 2 mL centrifuge tubes that had strong light shielding capabilities to ensure fluorescence only under UV light exposure. The tubes were consistently positioned during UV exposure and photography to minimize potential differences in light scattering. To address the limited sample size of the raw data, image augmentation techniques were employed to expand the dataset before the network training stage. This was done to avoid overfitting and improve the model's ability to generalize. Image augmentation is a common method used in deep learning and computer vision, which includes techniques such as cropping, horizontal flip, vertical flip, rotation, and padding. Among these techniques, image rotation augmentation generating new images by adjusting parameters like aspect ratio and rotation angle [47]. By increasing the training dataset through image augmentation, the issue of overfitting due to a small sample size is alleviated, resulting in improved generalization performance of the deep learning model and better results on the testing dataset [48].

We implemented image rotation augmentation to enrich the training dataset by horizontally and vertically flipping the original 250 images, resulting in an expanded 1000 images expanding dataset for model training. It is worth noting that the image augmentation method performs in this study only enlarge the image-concentration sample pairs which used for model training, and its aim is to train a more robust model, while the images utilized for concentration prediction remain the original real experimental images. The data preprocessing steps are illustrated in Fig. 7 , 250 images were loaded using the python opencv library, and the central regions of the images were selected as the target recognition areas, then the images were resized to 224 × 224 pixels through bilinear interpolation to match the network's input size. Subsequently, the image rotation augmentation technique was implemented by rotating the images clockwise to 90, 180, and 270°, thereby expanding the image-concentration sample pairs from 250 to 1000.

After data preprocessing, a deep learning prediction model was constructed. The deep learning algorithm proposed in this paper employs supervised training and consists of two main components: feature extraction and feature mapping. In the feature extraction stage, we build a network based on the AlexNet structure, utilizing five convolutional layers to progressively extract image features, from low-level to high-level abstractions. The feature extraction network takes as input a three-channel RGB image containing a convolutional layer, a rectified linear unit (ReLU) activation and a maximum pooling layer with a convolutional kernel size of 11 and 5 respectively. The details of parameters for each layer are illustrated in Table S1. Subsequently, the network is connected to two convolutional layers with kernel sizes of 3, and ReLU activation is applied after each convolutional layer. Then through a convolutional layer with kernel size of 3, a ReLU activation and a maxpooling layer to bring the image pixels to the feature space. The three-dimensional image features are then flattened into a one-dimensional vector by a flattening layer. Next, in the feature mapping stage, we build a fully-connected network consisting of five blocks, each block includes a dropout layer with a dropout rate of 0.5, a fully-connected layer, and a ReLU activation. The output dimensions of each fully-connected layers are 2048, 2048, 1024, 512, and 1, respectively. We use the last fully-connected layer to bring the output to the concentration space, and predict the concentration of fluorescent substances.

The algorithm was trained with total 1000 samples, each sample consisted of an image-concentration pair. For training and testing, the data set was divided in an 80-20 ratio. The algorithm was trained for 20 epochs with a learning rate of 0.0001 and a batch size of 16. The random state was set to 84. The supervised loss was measured by using mean squared error (MSE). After training, the algorithm was used to predict MTX concentration values.

Five independent experiments were used to assess the performance of the algorithm, yielding an average training time of 3.4 min and an average testing time of 0.10 s. After network finish training, the total time from capturing photos to predicting concentration values using the deep learning model is within 3 min, which is outperforming other studies listed in Table S3. The results of predicting MTX concentration are shown in Table S2.

Before measuring the spiked recoveries of actual samples in the optimal detection system, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) experiments were conducted to establish a standard detection method for MTX (related parameters were shown in Supplementary Information). The developed HPLC method exhibited exceptional detection performance, evidenced by an R2 value of 0.9989. Furthermore, no MTX was detected in the actual samples prior to the spiking recovery experiments. Subsequently, water samples were collected from the Shanxi Provincial Tumor Hospital, and the spiked recoveries of the actual samples were determined under the optimized detection conditions. As shown in Table S2, the N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs fluorescent nanosensor displayed recovery rates ranging from 98.25 % to 102.14 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 4 % (n = 3). The measured results were highly consistent with the actual spiked concentrations and HPLC analysis. In comparison, the N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs fluorescent nanosensor integrated with a deep learning algorithm achieved recovery rates between 99.73 % and 100.84 %, with an RSD below 3 %, further enhancing detection accuracy and stability. Additionally, the reliability of this method was further validated through comparison with HPLC results. Table S3 summarizes the detection performance of other commonly used fluorescent sensors for MTX. The results indicate that, compared to existing methods, the proposed approach demonstrates significant advantages in emission mode, linear range, detection limit, and detection time. In summary, the developed fluorescent nanosensor, when combined with a deep learning algorithm, enables highly accurate and reliable detection of MTX residues in environmental samples.

In this work, a novel nanoscale ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor (N-CDs@mSiO2-SH@AuAg NCs) is developed by using nanostructured N-CDs@mSiO2 as an internal reference signal, and nanoscale GSH@AuAg NCs binding to N-CDs@mSiO2-SH as a fluorescence response signal. Following the addition of MTX, the emission intensity of nanoscale GSH@AuAg NCs at 600 nm decreases, whereas the emission intensity of nanostructured N-CDs@mSiO2-SH at 460 nm increases. This change is also reflected in the fluorescence color, shifting from orange-red to blue when exposed to UV light, showcasing the nanoscale sensitivity of the system. Subsequently, a deep learning concentration prediction algorithm is proposed as an alternative to traditional methods of mobile phone photographic feature extraction and linear feature mapping. The deep learning algorithm combines with a nanoscale fluorescent nanosensor allows for end-to-end concentration prediction directly from experimental images taken in complex detection environments, with low time-complexity and high reliability.
